stem:
A 28-year-old G3P2 woman presents to the maternity assessment unit at 32 weeks gestation with a sudden 200 mL fresh bright red vaginal bleed at home that has now slowed. She is not in pain. Her two previous births were both caesareans for breech and arrest. She has had no abdominal trauma. Her partner reports that she was hanging washing when the bleed started.

On examination she is alert and pink. The uterus is soft and non-tender to palpation. She has not had a speculum examination in this unit yet. The mid-trimester morphology scan (booking the dates the booking ultrasound recorded at 20 weeks) reported a low-lying anterior placenta with a placental edge covering the internal os; a follow-up scan has not yet been performed. Vital signs are below.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 96, blood pressure 118/72, respiratory rate 16, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees, foetal heart rate 142 on intermittent Doppler
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 112 g/L, group and antibody screen on file (group O Rh negative, antibody negative), repeat group and screen and crossmatch 4 units sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent transvaginal ultrasound to localise the placenta, admit, give betamethasone 11.4 mg, and give anti-D 625 IU
options.rationale: Transvaginal ultrasound is the gold standard for placental localisation and is safer than digital examination in praevia. Admission, antenatal corticosteroids at 32 weeks (and again as part of a planned LSCS by 36 to 37 weeks), and anti-D prophylaxis in this Rh negative woman complete the package. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta

options.text: Digital cervical examination in the assessment room to determine cervical dilatation and station before any imaging
options.rationale: Digital cervical examination is contraindicated in suspected placenta praevia because of the risk of catastrophic haemorrhage. Imaging precedes examination. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta

options.text: Discharge home with weekly outpatient cardiotocography after the bleeding settles and plan vaginal birth at full term
options.rationale: A sentinel bleed in suspected placenta praevia requires inpatient admission. Vaginal birth is contraindicated when the placenta covers the internal os and elective caesarean is planned 36+0 to 37+0 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta

options.text: Proceed directly to category 1 caesarean section without imaging because of the painless fresh red vaginal bleeding
options.rationale: Maternal haemodynamic stability and a reassuring foetal heart trace allow time for imaging and corticosteroids. Category 1 caesarean is reserved for unstable mother or non-reassuring foetal status. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta

options.text: Insert a cervical tamponade balloon to reduce ongoing bleeding before further imaging or specialist obstetric review
options.rationale: Cervical balloon tamponade has no antepartum role in praevia; it is reserved for postpartum haemorrhage. Antepartum management is imaging, admission, steroids, anti-D, and planned LSCS. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta

explanation.summary: Painless fresh bright red bleeding with a soft non-tender uterus suggests placenta praevia, whereas constant pain, a hard tender uterus, and concealed bleeding suggest placental abruption. The two often coexist after vaginal manipulation, so digital examination is contraindicated until ultrasound has excluded praevia. Management of sentinel bleeds in known or suspected praevia involves admission, antenatal corticosteroids if before 34+6 weeks (and consideration of magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection under 30 weeks), and anti-D in Rh negative women. Elective caesarean is planned at 36+0 to 37+0 weeks unless bleeding forces earlier delivery.
key_points: Painless bright red bleeding plus soft uterus suggests praevia; painful tender uterus suggests abruption
Digital cervical examination is contraindicated until praevia excluded
Transvaginal ultrasound is safer and more accurate than transabdominal for placental localisation
Antenatal corticosteroids 24+0 to 34+6 weeks if delivery within 7 days is anticipated
Anti-D 625 IU IM in Rh negative women after any antepartum bleed (and any indicated post-event)
explanation.pearls: Two or more previous caesarean sections plus an anterior praevia raises placenta accreta spectrum risk above 40 percent - request MRI of the lower segment and plan delivery in a centre with interventional radiology and massive transfusion support.

sources.label: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Placenta Accreta

stem:
A 30-year-old G3P0+2 woman attends a high risk antenatal clinic at 18 weeks gestation. Her two previous pregnancies ended at 21 and 23 weeks with painless cervical dilatation and bulging membranes followed by spontaneous preterm birth and neonatal death. There is no history of cervical surgery or LLETZ. The current dating ultrasound was concordant with menstrual dates.

On examination the abdomen is soft and there is no contraction. A transvaginal ultrasound today shows a singleton with normal anatomy and a cervical length of 18 mm with funnelling at the internal os. There are no signs of intra-amniotic infection: she is afebrile, the maternal CRP is 4 mg/L, white cells 9.2 x10^9/L, and there is no discharge. The vaginal swab and urine culture are negative.

data_table.Investigations: cervical length 18 mm, funnelling at internal os, foetus appropriate for dates with normal anatomy, no membrane prolapse, CRP 4 mg/L (<5), white cells 9.2 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform an emergency rescue cervical cerclage today even though the membranes are not prolapsed and cervix is closed
options.rationale: Rescue cerclage is reserved for dilated cervix with prolapsing membranes (physical examination indicated). With closed cervix and short ultrasound length, ultrasound-indicated (not rescue) cerclage is appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Insert an Arabin cervical pessary and avoid surgical cerclage to minimise procedural and anaesthetic risk for her
options.rationale: Arabin pessary trials (PECEP, ProTWIN) have not consistently reduced preterm birth and Australian practice favours cerclage with this history. Pessary is a second line option when cerclage is contraindicated or declined. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Commence weekly intramuscular 17 hydroxyprogesterone caproate as monotherapy in the second and third trimester
options.rationale: Subsequent trials (PROLONG) failed to replicate the benefit of weekly intramuscular 17 OH-progesterone, and Australian guidance now prefers vaginal progesterone for short cervix. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Recommend strict bed rest in hospital until 34 weeks gestation without procedural intervention or progesterone
options.rationale: Bed rest does not reduce preterm birth and increases venous thromboembolism and deconditioning risk. It is not standard care for cervical insufficiency. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Schedule an ultrasound indicated transvaginal cervical cerclage and continue vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly
options.rationale: Two or more prior second trimester losses with painless cervical dilatation, current short cervix under 25 mm before 24 weeks, and no infection signs is a textbook indication for ultrasound-indicated cervical cerclage. Vaginal progesterone is added because the cervix is shorter than 25 mm. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency presents as painless cervical dilatation and second trimester loss. Three cerclage indications exist: history-indicated (three or more prior second trimester losses, place at 12 to 14 weeks), ultrasound-indicated (one or more prior preterm birth plus cervical length under 25 mm before 24 weeks), and physical examination-indicated (rescue) when membranes prolapse. Vaginal progesterone reduces preterm birth in women with cervical length under 25 mm regardless of history. Active intra-amniotic infection, vaginal bleeding, ruptured membranes, and active labour contraindicate cerclage.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks for 3 or more prior second trimester losses
Ultrasound-indicated cerclage for prior preterm birth plus cervical length < 25 mm before 24 weeks
Rescue cerclage for dilated cervix with prolapsing membranes
Vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly for short cervix (< 25 mm)
Remove cerclage at 36 to 37 weeks or earlier if labour, rupture of membranes, or chorioamnionitis
explanation.pearls: Ultrasound-indicated cerclage halves the preterm birth rate (number needed to treat about 8) in singleton pregnancies with prior preterm birth and cervical length under 25 mm - the benefit is lost in twin pregnancies.

stem:
A 31-year-old G2P1 woman with an in-vitro fertilisation singleton pregnancy reaches 35 plus 4 weeks gestation. Her morphology scan reported a velamentous cord insertion with a bilobed placenta, and a third trimester scan at 32 weeks confirmed a vessel running across the internal cervical os without supporting placental tissue. She has been admitted at 34 weeks for surveillance and completed a course of antenatal corticosteroids.

This afternoon she has a brisk fresh red vaginal bleed of about 100 mL after her membranes ruptured spontaneously. The midwife describes the liquor as port-wine coloured. She has no abdominal pain and the uterus is soft. Cardiotocography shows a baseline of 110 falling to 90 with a sinusoidal pattern over the past four minutes. Her vital signs are stable.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 92, blood pressure 122/76, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees, foetal heart rate 110 with sinusoidal pattern
data_table.Investigations: maternal full blood count and coagulation within normal limits, group and screen on file, Apt test of vaginal blood positive for foetal haemoglobin

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Augment labour with intravenous oxytocin aiming for vaginal birth given the gestation of 35 plus 4 weeks today
options.rationale: Augmentation accelerates foetal blood loss through the torn vasa praevia and is contraindicated. Vasa praevia is an absolute indication for caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Immediate category 1 caesarean section with the neonatal resuscitation team and uncrossmatched O negative blood ready
options.rationale: Confirmed vasa praevia with rupture of membranes producing port-wine vaginal blood, a positive Apt test (foetal haemoglobin), and a sinusoidal CTG indicates foetal exsanguination from a torn velamentous vessel. Category 1 caesarean is the only intervention that prevents foetal death; antenatal diagnosis converts mortality from 60 percent to under 5 percent. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Insert an intrauterine pressure catheter and observe with continuous cardiotocography over the next sixty minutes
options.rationale: Sinusoidal cardiotocography indicates established foetal anaemia and ongoing observation risks foetal death. Definitive delivery is the only treatment. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Administer maternal intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g and reassess the foetal heart trace in fifteen minutes' time
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid does not address foetal vessel rupture and delays definitive delivery. The bleeding is foetal in origin, not maternal. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Repeat the Apt test on vaginal blood in 15 minutes and proceed to caesarean only if it is positive a second time
options.rationale: The Apt test is already positive and the CTG is sinusoidal; further confirmation wastes time when foetal exsanguination is occurring. Caesarean must proceed immediately. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia is the presence of foetal vessels within the membranes overlying the internal os, usually from velamentous cord insertion or accessory placental lobes. Antenatal diagnosis (by colour Doppler transvaginal ultrasound) allows planned caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks and converts mortality from 60 to 90 percent (undiagnosed, rupture of membranes setting) to under 5 percent. Risk factors include IVF pregnancies, low-lying or bilobed placentas, velamentous insertion, and multiple pregnancy. The Apt test distinguishes foetal from maternal blood at the bedside (foetal haemoglobin is alkali-resistant).
key_points: Antenatal diagnosis converts mortality from > 60 percent to under 5 percent
Risk factors: IVF, low-lying placenta, velamentous cord, bilobed/succenturiate placenta
Plan elective LSCS at 34 to 36 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids
Sinusoidal CTG plus port-wine vaginal blood after rupture of membranes is foetal exsanguination
Apt test (alkali denaturation) at the bedside distinguishes foetal from maternal blood
explanation.pearls: Vasa praevia type 1 follows velamentous cord insertion within the lower segment; type 2 connects two placental lobes; both warrant planned 34 to 36 week LSCS after corticosteroids.

subtopic_detail: Selective foetal growth restriction in MCDA twins
stem:
A 27-year-old G2P1 woman with a monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA) twin pregnancy is reviewed at the maternal foetal medicine clinic at 26 plus 5 weeks. Both twins were grown concordantly at the 18 week scan. The 24 week scan showed an estimated foetal weight discordance of 22 percent. Today's scan shows further discordance.

Twin A is appropriately grown on the 35th centile with normal umbilical artery Doppler. Twin B is on the 4th centile with an intermittent absent end-diastolic flow pattern in the umbilical artery that cycles between forward, absent, and reversed (positive then negative then positive) every several minutes when watched for 15 minutes. The middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocity is within normal limits in both twins. Amniotic fluid volumes are normal in both sacs. Maternal blood pressure is normal and there is no proteinuria.

data_table.Investigations: twin A estimated foetal weight 35th centile, umbilical artery PI within normal limits; twin B estimated foetal weight 4th centile, intermittent absent and reversed end-diastolic flow (Gratacos type III); middle cerebral artery peak systolic velocities within normal limits in both; amniotic fluid normal in both sacs; no anatomical anomalies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section at 36 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids and weekly outpatient surveillance
options.rationale: Gratacos type III selective IUGR carries an unpredictable risk of sudden foetal death of twin B (around 15 percent) and 20 percent neurological injury in twin A from acute fetofoetal transfusion at the moment of death. Awaiting 36 weeks is unsafe. (Source: ISUOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Refer to a tertiary maternal foetal medicine unit for foetoscopic laser ablation or selective bipolar cord occlusion
options.rationale: Gratacos type III selective IUGR (intermittent absent/reversed end-diastolic flow) reflects a single large arterio-arterial anastomosis; spontaneous demise of twin B carries around 20 percent risk of neurological injury to twin A from acute hypotension across the anastomosis. Tertiary foetoscopic dichorionisation by laser ablation or selective reduction by bipolar cord occlusion are the recognised options. (Source: ISUOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer maternal oral sildenafil to augment placental perfusion in the growth restricted twin from this week
options.rationale: The STRIDER trial of maternal sildenafil for severe early onset IUGR was halted because of neonatal pulmonary hypertension and excess deaths. Sildenafil is not recommended. (Source: ISUOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence weekly outpatient cardiotocography and repeat umbilical artery Doppler assessment again in two weeks
options.rationale: Weekly intermittent surveillance is inadequate for the unpredictable cycling Doppler pattern of Gratacos type III, which can precede acute demise within days. Inpatient or tertiary referral is needed. (Source: ISUOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Deliver both twins by emergency caesarean section today at 26 plus 5 weeks regardless of viability considerations
options.rationale: Pre-viable iatrogenic preterm delivery to rescue twin B sacrifices both twins to extreme prematurity (around 35 percent mortality at 26 weeks and high major morbidity). Tertiary intervention to dichorionise the placentae is preferred. (Source: ISUOG Multiple Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Selective foetal growth restriction (sFGR) in MCDA twins is classified by umbilical artery Doppler in the growth restricted twin: type I (positive end-diastolic flow), type II (persistent absent or reversed end-diastolic flow), and type III (intermittent absent or reversed end-diastolic flow). Type III is the highest risk because of unpredictable sudden death of twin B and around 20 percent risk of neurological injury to the larger co-twin from acute exsanguination across a single large arterio-arterial anastomosis. Tertiary management options include foetoscopic laser dichorionisation or selective bipolar cord occlusion; both convert MCDA to a functional dichorionic pregnancy at the placental level.
key_points: Gratacos classification of sFGR in MCDA twins (type I, II, III)
Type III intermittent absent/reversed UA EDF: 15 percent demise risk, 20 percent neuro injury risk in co-twin
Tertiary options: foetoscopic laser ablation, selective bipolar cord occlusion
Maternal sildenafil contraindicated (STRIDER trial harm signal)
Pre-viable iatrogenic delivery causes worse outcomes than expectant management at tertiary centre
explanation.pearls: In Gratacos type III selective FGR a single large arterio-arterial anastomosis carries the cycling Doppler pattern; foetoscopic laser ablation converts the placenta functionally to dichorionic and removes the catastrophic 20 percent neurological injury risk to the larger twin if its co-twin dies.

stem:
A 28-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance two hours after a witnessed overdose. Her partner found an empty pack of dothiepin 75 mg tablets (originally 25 tablets) and a half empty bottle of red wine. She is drowsy with a Glasgow Coma Score of 11. She has had no seizures pre-hospital. There is no history of cardiac disease.

On arrival a 12-lead ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 128, a QRS duration of 132 milliseconds with a rightward terminal R wave in aVR of 4 millimetres, and a QTc of 480 milliseconds. Her pupils are dilated, mouth is dry, and bladder palpable. Auscultation is clear. Capillary glucose 5.2 mmol/L. There is no focal neurology. Vital signs are below.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 128, blood pressure 96/58, respiratory rate 14, SpO2 97 percent on room air, temperature 36.4 degrees
data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas pH 7.34, lactate 2.4 mmol/L, potassium 3.6 mmol/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, urinary urine drug screen positive for tricyclics, paracetamol level pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous flumazenil 200 micrograms to reverse co-ingested benzodiazepine sedation and improve airway control
options.rationale: Flumazenil precipitates seizures in TCA overdose by removing the protective benzodiazepine effect on a pro-convulsant brain; it is specifically contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

options.text: Intravenous 8.4 percent sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus and repeat to target QRS under 100 ms and pH 7.50 to 7.55
options.rationale: QRS prolongation above 100 milliseconds and the R wave in aVR of 3 mm or more are markers of sodium channel blockade and predict seizures and ventricular arrhythmia. Sodium bicarbonate provides both a sodium load (overwhelms blockade) and alkalinisation (increases protein binding) and is the first line intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

options.text: Intravenous amiodarone 300 mg over 20 minutes to treat the broad complex tachycardia and prevent ventricular arrhythmia
options.rationale: Class Ia, Ic, and III antiarrhythmics further block sodium channels or prolong QT in TCA overdose and may precipitate arrest. Sodium bicarbonate is the antiarrhythmic of choice here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube now without prior airway protection given the rising drowsiness
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is contraindicated without a protected airway in a patient with a falling GCS; risk of aspiration outweighs benefit at two hours post-ingestion. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes to address the prolonged QTc and prevent torsades de pointes
options.rationale: Magnesium addresses torsades de pointes but is not the priority while sodium channel blockade continues. Bicarbonate addresses both QRS and QTc by reversing the sodium channel block. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes sodium channel blockade (QRS widening, R wave in aVR over 3 mm, ventricular arrhythmia), anticholinergic syndrome, alpha-1 blockade (hypotension), and seizures. Treatment is sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg IV bolus repeated to target QRS less than 100 ms and arterial pH 7.50 to 7.55, intubation if GCS falls below 9 or seizures occur, benzodiazepines (not phenytoin) for seizures, and noradrenaline for refractory hypotension. Intravenous lipid emulsion is considered for cardiac arrest unresponsive to standard measures. Flumazenil is contraindicated.
key_points: QRS > 100 ms predicts seizures; QRS > 160 ms predicts ventricular arrhythmia
R wave in aVR > 3 mm is a sensitive marker of sodium channel blockade
Sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg IV bolus, target QRS < 100 ms and pH 7.50 to 7.55
Benzodiazepines (not phenytoin) for TCA seizures
Flumazenil contraindicated; activated charcoal only if airway protected and within 1 to 2 hours
explanation.pearls: Intravenous lipid emulsion 20 percent at 1.5 mL/kg over 1 minute then 0.25 mL/kg/min may rescue refractory cardiac arrest in lipophilic TCA toxicity - sequester the drug in the lipid phase.

stem:
A 27-year-old long-haul truck driver presents to a community psychiatry clinic for assessment of suspected attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. He has had chronic difficulties with concentration, planning, finishing tasks, and emotional regulation since primary school. He has accumulated three speeding infringements and two near-miss incidents at work in the past 18 months. The screening Adult ADHD Self Report Scale and a collateral history from his mother are highly consistent with combined-type ADHD; structured clinical interview confirms the diagnosis.

He has no history of psychosis, no current substance use disorder, no cardiac disease, blood pressure 122/78, and a normal baseline ECG. He drinks two standard drinks twice a week. He is keen to start medication so he can keep his commercial driver's licence. He has been informed that his diagnosis has driving implications.

data_table.Investigations: ECG within normal limits, blood pressure 122/78, urinary drug screen negative, full blood count and liver function within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence bupropion 150 mg daily as a stimulant alternative with no driver licensing notification required of him
options.rationale: Bupropion is not first line for ADHD in Australia and the driving notification requirement is triggered by the diagnosis, not by the drug class. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guideline)

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 40 mg daily and reassure him that no driver licensing notification is required at present
options.rationale: Notification of a new ADHD diagnosis is mandatory for commercial driver license holders under Austroads regardless of medication choice. Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant second line option, not first line. (Source: Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive)

options.text: Refer for cognitive behavioural therapy alone and avoid pharmacotherapy because of the commercial driving implications
options.rationale: CBT is adjunctive but does not match the effect size of stimulants in adult ADHD. Withholding pharmacotherapy from a symptomatic adult on the basis of driving risk is paternalistic and not supported by guideline. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guideline)

options.text: Commence modafinil 100 mg daily as an off-label alternative to stimulants for adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder
options.rationale: Modafinil is not recommended for ADHD in Australian guidelines and is approved for narcolepsy and shift work sleep disorder. Stimulants remain first line. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guideline)

options.text: Commence dexamfetamine 5 mg twice daily under psychiatry authority and notify the state driver licensing authority
options.rationale: Long acting or short acting psychostimulants (dexamfetamine, methylphenidate, lisdexamfetamine) are first line for adult ADHD; in Australia they are S8 medicines requiring psychiatry-led prescription. Under Austroads 'Assessing Fitness to Drive', a commercial driver with a new ADHD diagnosis must notify the licensing authority and is generally fit to drive only on stable treatment with documented benefit. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guideline)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD treatment in Australia combines stimulant pharmacotherapy (dexamfetamine, methylphenidate, lisdexamfetamine) with psychoeducation and CBT. Stimulants are Schedule 8 drugs and prescribing is restricted to psychiatrists or paediatricians under state and territory authority. Atomoxetine and guanfacine are non-stimulant options. Under Austroads 'Assessing Fitness to Drive', a commercial driver with a new ADHD diagnosis is required to notify the driver licensing authority; private drivers are not generally required to notify but the doctor is expected to advise the patient on safe driving and impairment. Stable treatment with documented response usually allows return to commercial driving.
key_points: Stimulants (methylphenidate, dexamfetamine, lisdexamfetamine) are first line
Atomoxetine, guanfacine are non-stimulant options
S8 medicines: psychiatry / paediatric authority prescribing in Australia
Austroads: commercial drivers must notify; private drivers advised by doctor
Baseline ECG, BP, weight monitoring during titration
explanation.pearls: Lisdexamfetamine has a smoother pharmacokinetic profile than dexamfetamine because it is a prodrug requiring red cell hydrolysis to release dexamfetamine - this caps the rate of release and reduces euphoric peak, supporting once daily dosing.
